title: Nest box management and kit checking
subtitle: The twice-daily check that keeps a litter alive.
excerpt: A meat rabbit doe can deliver 8 to 12 kits and lose half of them to cold, mismothering, or unnoticed wounds before day 10 if no one is looking. What to check each morning and evening, how to handle very young kits safely, and when to act.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Rabbits (2009). Cornell Small Farms Programme, meat rabbit management. University of Wisconsin Extension, rabbit husbandry series. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (2012 edition), rabbits chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: Newborn and very young rabbits, from birth to approximately 4 weeks of age. Born blind, deaf, and hairless; dependent on nest warmth and milk from the doe for survival.
The act of a doe giving birth. Normal kindling takes 15 to 30 minutes for a full litter. Does may kindle at night; by morning the litter will be in the nest box if the doe is settled.
An adult female rabbit. A mature meat breed doe can kindle 5 to 7 litters per year; smaller-scale operations run 4 to 5 to allow adequate rest between litters.

When a doe fails to nurse her litter, either through insufficient milk, inexperience, or stress. First-time does are more likely to mismother; good nest box management and minimal disturbance around kindling time reduces the risk.

content.text: The nest box is the highest-risk point in the rabbit operation.
Kits
are born without thermoregulation and depend entirely on the warmth of the nest and their littermates for survival in the first 10 days. A twice-daily check from
kindling
to weaning catches dead kits before they contaminate the nest, confirms the
doe
is nursing, and identifies problems before they become losses.
Before you open the nest box

Rub your hands in the nesting material from the outside of the box for a few seconds before touching the kits. This transfers the smell of the nest onto your hands and reduces the chance that human scent disturbs the doe when you replace the kits. A settled, experienced doe will not reject kits handled by the keeper. A first-time or stressed doe may be less reliable; minimise handling time if she is showing signs of agitation.
The daily check

Open the lid or front panel of the nest box and part the nesting material with your fingers. Count the kits. Every kit should be wriggling or at rest in a pile with the others; a kit lying alone at the edge of the nest is cold and may be in distress. Remove any dead kits immediately; a decomposing body in the nest increases the risk of infection to the survivors.
Count kits and compare to yesterday's count
Check for kits outside the nest box or on the cage floor (remove promptly and warm if cool but alive)
Check that surviving kits have rounded, full bellies (a sign that the doe is nursing normally)
Check the nest material is dry; replace immediately if wet
Check the doe is eating and drinking; a doe not eating after kindling may be unwell

attrs.title: What a well-nursed kit looks like
attrs.body: A kit that is being nursed has a visibly rounded, slightly distended belly. The skin, while still hairless in the first few days, looks plump rather than wrinkled. A kit with a sunken belly and wrinkled skin is not getting enough milk. Does nurse their kits once or twice in a 24-hour period, usually in the early morning. The nursing session is brief; you are unlikely to observe it directly.

content.text: Warming a cold kit

A kit that is cold, limp, and barely moving can often be revived if it is properly chilled rather than dead. Cup it in both hands and breathe warm air over it for several minutes. Once it starts to wriggle actively, return it to the centre of the nest pile. A kit that remains limp after 10 minutes of warming is unlikely to survive.
Managing the nest box through to weaning

Kits begin leaving the nest box around 10 days of age, when their eyes open, and are eating solid food alongside nursing by 3 weeks. The nest box can be removed at 4 weeks of age. By this point the kits are fully mobile and the box takes up space that the growing litter needs.
If there is a shortage of
mismothering

or a doe with no milk, a foster doe who has recently kindled herself can sometimes take an extra kit or two. Place the fostered kit among her litter and add a pinch of the foster doe's nest material to mask the scent. Monitor closely for the first 12 hours.

items.symptom: Kits scattered around the cage floor rather than in the nest box
items.cause: The doe kindled outside the nest box, or a kit fell out and the doe did not retrieve it
items.fix: Return the kits to the nest box immediately. If the doe kindled outside the box, transfer all the kits and nesting material together. Warm any cold kits first.

items.symptom: Kits have thin, wrinkled bellies and are weak by day 3
items.cause: Doe has insufficient milk production, or is not nursing regularly
items.fix: Check the doe's nipples: they should be slightly enlarged and clearly defined. A doe with mastitis (hot, hard, discoloured gland) is not producing usable milk; call your vet. A healthy doe not nursing well may be stressed by location or neighbour; move her to a quieter spot.

items.cause: Cannibalism in stressed or first-time does. Common triggers: a dead or injured kit in the nest, or mineral deficiency.
items.fix: Remove any dead kits immediately. Provide adequate mineral supplementation and ensure the doe is not stressed by foot traffic or nearby predators. A first-time doe that cannibalises a whole litter may do so again. Whether to retain her for breeding is your call.
